planting.note: Plant in spring into a sunny border with free-draining soil.

directions: Plant in a well-drained bed and stake taller stems in exposed sites. This classic selection bears the deepest violet-blue flowers of the group with contrasting golden centers in early summer. Deadhead spent stems to prolong the display and prevent flopping.

planting.note: Start seed indoors in late winter or plant nursery stock in spring.

directions: Surface-sow and keep lightly moist, as this cultivar comes fairly true from seed. Soft lilac-pink daisies with yellow eyes cover the clump through early summer. Cut back after flowering to keep the plant compact and coax a second show.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun and moderately fertile soil.

directions: Set plants into well-drained ground and give them room to form a tidy clump. Semi-double deep rose-pink daisies open over a long stretch of early summer. Shear the whole plant back after the main flush to encourage fresh flowering stems.

planting.note: Plant in spring into a sunny, well-drained border.

directions: Plant at the original soil depth and mulch lightly to conserve moisture. Nearly double pale mauve-blue daisies smother the clump in early summer. Remove faded flowers and the plant often obliges with a lighter second flush.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun with good drainage.

directions: Set plants into free-draining soil and water until they are established. Buds open pale pink and fade to near-white, giving a soft two-tone effect over the summer clump. Cut back after flowering to tidy the foliage and support rebloom.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a sunny bed with average soil.

directions: Plant into well-drained ground and give the clump space to breathe. Lavender-violet daisies with fine rays and yellow eyes open freely in early summer. Deadhead the spent stems to keep the plant looking fresh and to prolong bloom.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun and well-drained soil.

directions: Set plants at the depth they grew in the pot and firm the soil gently. White rayed daisies with soft yellow centers stand above the clump in early summer. Shear after flowering to keep the habit neat and to invite a second flush.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a sunny, free-draining position.

directions: Plant into loosened, well-drained soil and water in thoroughly. Large single lavender-blue daisies with narrow rays crowd the stems in early summer. Cut the clump back after the main display to encourage tidy regrowth and further bloom.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun and well-drained soil.

directions: Set plants into free-draining ground and mulch lightly around the crown. Rich reddish-pink daisies with golden eyes are among the deepest colors in the group. Deadhead regularly and shear after the first flush to keep flowers coming.

planting.note: Start seed indoors in late winter or divide clumps in spring.

directions: Surface-sow the seed and press it into moist soil, leaving it uncovered for light. This eastern North American native forms softly hairy basal rosettes that spread by runners into a groundcover. Pale lavender to white daisies rise on slender stems in mid to late spring; it tolerates part shade better than most fleabanes.

planting.note: Plant in spring in part sun into moist, well-drained soil.

directions: Plant into humus-rich soil where the low rosettes can knit into a dense mat by their stolons. This vigorous selection makes an excellent living groundcover under high shade. Pale blue-white daisies float above the foliage in spring and the mat suppresses weeds thereafter.

planting.note: Sow indoors after a cold spell or sow outdoors in autumn.

directions: Give seed a period of cold moist chilling to improve germination, then keep it barely covered. This tiny alpine forms cushions of finely divided foliage well suited to troughs and rock crevices. Solitary white to pale lavender daisies sit just above the mound in late spring and demand sharp drainage.

planting.note: Cold-stratify seed and start indoors in late winter.

directions: Chill the seed before sowing and press it onto a gritty surface without burying it. This dwarf mountain fleabane makes low tufts of soft gray-green leaves. Bright golden-yellow daisies, unusual for the genus, open on short stems in early summer and need excellent drainage to thrive.

planting.note: Plant in spring into a gritty trough or rock garden pocket.

directions: Plant into very sharply drained, gritty soil and top-dress with stone chips. This selection reliably produces clear yellow daisies over neat low tufts through early summer. Keep it lean and dry, as rich wet soil rots the crown.

planting.note: Direct-sow in spring or fall onto moist, disturbed soil.

directions: Scatter seed on the surface of moist soil and do not cover, as it needs light to sprout. This adaptable native carries clusters of small pink-tinged white daisies packed with fine rays in late spring. It self-sows readily in meadows and moist ground and is a valuable early nectar source.

planting.note: Direct-sow in spring onto open, sunny ground.

directions: Sow seed on the surface and press it in without covering, keeping the ground moist until seedlings appear. This tall annual or short-lived plant produces airy sprays of small white daisies with yellow centers all summer. It reseeds prolifically, so deadhead if you want to limit its spread in a border.

planting.note: Direct-sow in spring in a dry, sunny site.

directions: Sow seed shallowly onto lean, well-drained soil in spring. This western native bears masses of small daisies with many fine white to pale lavender rays over a long, dry season. It is drought-tolerant and reseeds freely, making it useful in wild and xeric plantings.

planting.note: Plant in spring into well-drained soil in full sun.

directions: Set plants into gritty, free-draining soil where the low mound can spread slowly. This tough hybrid blooms exceptionally long, with lavender daisies fading to pale pink over green foliage. It shrugs off drought once established and needs only a shear to refresh it.

planting.note: Plant in spring into a sunny, sharply drained rock garden.

directions: Plant into gritty, lean soil and top-dress with grit to keep the crown dry. This low, mat-forming selection is smothered in bright yellow daisies in late spring and early summer. Give it full sun and sharp drainage, and shear lightly after bloom.

planting.note: Plant in spring in full sun and average, well-drained soil.

directions: Set plants at the original depth and give them room for good airflow. Soft pink daisies with fine rays are produced generously over a long early-summer season. Cut the clump back after the first flush and it typically reblooms into late summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring into a sunny border with free-draining soil.

directions: Plant into well-drained ground and stake if the site is windy. Deep violet-blue daisies with golden centers open freely across early summer. Deadhead spent stems and shear after the main flush to prolong the display.

planting.note: Start seed indoors after a period of cold-moist chilling, or set out young plants after frost.

directions: Barely cover the seed and give it several weeks of cold, damp refrigeration to break dormancy before warmth. Grow in lean, sharply drained soil and avoid rich feeding, which causes floppy stems. Steel-blue branched heads open in mid to late summer and dry well for arrangements.

planting.note: Sow fresh seed in autumn so winter cold triggers spring germination in place.

directions: Scatter seed on open, gritty ground in fall since it resents transplanting from its long taproot. This short-lived monocarpic plant blooms in its second year with huge silvery bracts, then dies after setting seed. Leave a few dried heads to self-sow and keep the colony going.

planting.note: Chill seed cold and moist, then start indoors before setting out after frost.

directions: Sow shallowly after cold stratification and plant into fast-draining, gravelly soil. The deeply cut leaves carry silver-white veins, and silvery-blue cones rise on branched stems in summer. Cut a few stems just as the color deepens for long-lasting dried bunches.

planting.note: Cold-stratify seed, then start indoors and transplant out after the last frost.

directions: Give this species cool, well-drained soil and full sun, and avoid disturbing the taproot once established. The large flower cones are ringed by an unusually soft, feathery blue ruff of finely divided bracts. Enjoy the steel-blue heads from midsummer, when they are also prized by pollinators.

planting.note: Sow fresh seed in fall into sandy soil; germination is slow and erratic.

directions: This true coastal species wants pure sand or very gritty, sharply drained soil and full sun. Rounded blue-gray leaves and pale powder-blue heads suit seaside and gravel gardens. Handle the long taproot with care, since established plants strongly resent being moved.

planting.note: Start cold-treated seed indoors and set plants out once frost has passed.

directions: Prized for its evergreen rosette of dark leaves boldly marbled with white veins, this species stays tidy year-round. Small silvery-blue cones with spiny bracts rise on wiry stems in summer. Plant in lean, well-drained soil and avoid winter wet, which is its main weakness.

planting.note: Start seed indoors in warmth and transplant after all frost risk.

directions: This bold South American species forms a rosette of long, sword-like, saw-toothed evergreen leaves. Tall branched stems carry greenish-white thimble-shaped flower heads through summer. Give it full sun and good drainage, and mulch the crown where winters dip toward its cold limit.

planting.note: Sow indoors in warmth and plant out young rosettes after frost.

directions: Arching, narrow spiny leaves form a fountain-like clump, above which tall stems hold small ivory-green flower balls. Its airy, see-through habit works well toward the middle of a sunny border. Provide sharp drainage and full sun, and protect the crown in colder gardens.

planting.note: Start indoors and move out after frost; needs a long season to establish.

directions: This giant species makes a dramatic evergreen clump of long, strappy, finely toothed leaves. Towering branched stems bear masses of tiny maroon-purple flower heads in late summer and fall. Give it a warm, sheltered spot with rich but well-drained soil and shelter from hard frost.

planting.note: Direct-sow into warm soil after the last frost where plants are to bloom.

directions: Unlike its perennial cousins, this North American native is a true annual grown fresh each year from seed. Sow directly in lean, dry soil and full sun, since it thrives on neglect and heat. Vivid amethyst-purple crowns top spiny stems in late summer, glowing against the drying prairie.

planting.note: Fall-sow or cold-stratify seed before spring sowing in dry ground.

directions: A rugged Eurasian species of dry grassland with much-divided, spiny gray-green foliage. Pale greenish-white flower heads appear in summer, and dead plants can break off to tumble like a small tumbleweed. Grow it in poor, sharply drained soil and full sun for the best form.

planting.note: Start seed indoors in warmth and set out after frost in a sunny spot.

directions: Sometimes called Mexican thistle, this striking species carries large heads ringed by long, silvery, spine-tipped bracts that resemble a protea. Grow in gritty, free-draining soil in full sun and shelter it from hard freezes. The bold flowers appear in summer and make excellent long-lasting cut stems.

planting.note: Set out nursery plants or root cuttings in spring, since this hybrid does not come true from seed.

directions: This garden hybrid group is grown for its exceptionally large, intense blue flower heads and matching blue-flushed stems. Because it does not breed true, buy named plants or propagate by root cuttings rather than seed. Plant in lean, well-drained soil and full sun, and enjoy the vivid cones through midsummer.

planting.note: Cold-stratify seed, start indoors, then plant out after frost.

directions: This compact dwarf selection stays low and bushy, making it ideal for the front of a border or a large container. Give it the same lean, gritty, well-drained soil as the species and full sun. Steel-blue heads on short branched stems appear in summer and dry beautifully.

planting.note: Start chilled seed indoors and transplant out after the last frost.

directions: The name means blue cap, and this selection is valued for its deep blue, freely branching flower heads. Grow it in poor, fast-draining soil and full sun to keep the stems strong and self-supporting. Cut stems at peak color for fresh or dried arrangements, or leave them for pollinators.

planting.note: Start seed early indoors after chilling; this strain can bloom its first year from an early sowing.

directions: This uniform seed strain is popular with cut-flower growers for its bright silvery-blue heads and strong straight stems. Start it early indoors so it can reach flowering size and bloom in its first summer. Grow in lean, well-drained soil and full sun, and harvest stems when the color has fully developed.

planting.note: Set out nursery-grown plants in spring; the variegation is not stable from seed.

directions: This variegated selection is grown mainly for foliage edged in creamy white that flushes pink in cool weather. Because the variegation does not come true from seed, buy plants or propagate by division or root cuttings. Give it full sun and sharp drainage, and blue-tinted flower heads follow in summer.

planting.note: Cold-stratify seed, start indoors, and move out after frost.

directions: A robust tetraploid selection with especially dark violet-blue flower heads and sturdy stems. Grow in poor, freely draining soil in full sun to keep the plant compact and upright. Blooms open in mid to late summer and are excellent for both fresh cutting and drying.

planting.note: Plant nursery stock or root cuttings in spring, as this hybrid is propagated vegetatively.

directions: This standout hybrid produces very large, richly colored blue cones above deep blue stems. Set plants in lean, sharply drained soil and full sun, and avoid rich feeding that softens the stems. The intense summer flowers are magnets for bees and dry well for arrangements.

planting.note: Set out plants or root cuttings in spring; does not come true from seed.

directions: Sold in the cut-flower trade for its bold, spiny blue heads and strong branching stems. Grow in poor, gritty, well-drained soil in full sun and propagate by root cuttings rather than seed. Cut stems at full color in summer for long-lasting fresh or dried displays.

planting.note: Plant nursery-grown stock in spring; this award-winning hybrid is grown vegetatively.

directions: This distinctive selection combines golden-yellow foliage and stems with blue flower heads for a two-tone effect. Give it full sun and lean, sharply drained soil, and shelter foliage from the harshest midday scorch in hot climates. The colorful cones appear in summer and hold well when cut.

planting.note: Cold-stratify seed, start indoors, and transplant after frost.

directions: This alpine selection is admired for its large cones surrounded by a soft, deeply cut blue collar. Grow in cool, well-drained soil in full sun and leave the taproot undisturbed once planted. The intense steel-blue heads open in midsummer and are excellent for drying.

planting.note: Start cold-treated seed indoors and set out after the last frost.

directions: This selection leans toward richer amethyst-violet tones in its large ruffed flower heads. Provide full sun and cool, free-draining soil, and avoid heavy feeding that weakens the stems. Cut the striking cones in summer for long-lasting fresh or dried arrangements.

planting.note: Cold-stratify seed, start indoors, then plant out after frost.

directions: Selected for larger, showier heads with an especially full feathery blue ruff. Grow in cool, gritty, well-drained soil in full sun and keep the crown free of winter wet. The bold blue cones appear in midsummer and are a favorite for cutting.

planting.note: Start chilled seed indoors and set out young plants after frost.

directions: This selection is prized for unusually deep, intense blue stems and flower cones over silver-veined leaves. Grow in lean, gravelly, sharply drained soil in full sun for the strongest color. The vivid blue heads appear in summer and hold their color well when dried.

planting.note: Cold-stratify seed, start indoors, then plant out after frost.

directions: A compact selection from the Picos de Europa with strongly silver-veined foliage and clear blue heads. Give it full sun and fast-draining, gritty soil, and avoid winter wet around the crown. The steel-blue cones open in summer and are excellent for small dried bunches.

planting.note: Start cold-treated seed indoors and transplant after the last frost.

directions: This selection is noted for richly silvered, spiny foliage and strongly blue-toned flower heads. Grow it in poor, sharply drained soil and full sun to bring out the best leaf and stem color. The blue cones appear through summer and are much visited by bees.

planting.note: Sow fresh seed in fall where plants are to grow, since the taproot dislikes moving.

directions: This selection offers especially bright, narrow silver bracts around the flower cones for a ghostly glow. Like the species it is short-lived and monocarpic, blooming in its second year and then dying after seeding. Let a few heads self-sow in gritty, sunny ground to keep the display going year after year.

planting.note: Set out nursery plants or root cuttings in spring; this hybrid is grown vegetatively.

directions: This popular hybrid is grown for its saturated sapphire-blue stems and matching flower cones. Plant in lean, sharply drained soil in full sun and propagate from root cuttings since it will not come true from seed. The vivid summer heads attract pollinators and dry well for arrangements.

planting.note: Start chilled seed indoors and set out young rosettes after frost.

directions: This selection is grown for its boldly white-marbled evergreen rosettes that stand out even out of bloom. Small silvery-blue cones with spiny bracts rise on wiry stems in summer. Plant in lean, gritty, well-drained soil and full sun, and protect it from winter wet.

planting.note: Fall-sow or cold-stratify seed before spring sowing in moist ground.

directions: Unlike its dry-loving relatives, this eastern North American native grows in wet meadows and pond margins. Give it consistently moist to boggy soil in full sun rather than the sharp drainage most sea hollies want. Pale blue-tinged globe flowers on branched stems appear in late summer and draw many pollinators.

planting.note: Cold-stratify seed over winter; this is the most compact of the wild Joe-Pyes.

directions: Scatter seed on damp ground and firm it in lightly without covering. This shorter species suits smaller gardens yet still offers rounded rosy flower clusters over dark stems. Blooms open in mid to late summer and are followed by fluffy seedheads that feed songbirds.

planting.note: Sow in fall or stratify; seed needs light and consistent moisture to sprout.

directions: Surface-sow onto wet, rich soil and keep the seedbed damp through germination. Distinctive paired leaves fuse around the stem, and flat clusters of fuzzy white flowers cap the plant. Bloom runs from midsummer into fall, supporting a wide range of small native pollinators.

planting.note: Cold-stratify or fall-sow; tolerates lean, dry soils once established.

directions: Surface-sow into well-drained ground and keep barely moist until sprouts appear. Unlike its wetland cousins this species shrugs off dry prairies and roadsides. Clusters of small white flowers open in late summer and hold into autumn.

planting.note: Sow into sandy, well-drained soil; this species thrives where others fail from drought.

directions: Scatter seed on lean, gritty ground and press in without burying. Fine, needle-like leaves give the plant an airy texture below flat heads of white bloom. Flowers appear in late summer and are especially good in dry sandy gardens and meadows.

planting.note: Cold-stratify seed; tolerates poor sandy soil and partial shade.

directions: Surface-sow onto well-drained ground and keep lightly moist until germination. Toothed leaves and bract-tipped buds give it a crisp look before the bright white flowers open. Bloom comes in mid to late summer and suits dry woodland edges and sandy borders.

planting.note: Sow in autumn or after a cold spell; a European native for damp ground.

directions: Press seed onto moist, fertile soil at a streamside or pond margin. The hemp-like foliage sets off fuzzy heads of dusky pink flowers loved by bees and butterflies. Bloom runs from midsummer to early autumn on this classic European wetland perennial.

planting.note: Cold-stratify seed; adaptable to sandy soils and light shade.

directions: Surface-sow onto lightly moist ground and keep from drying while seedlings emerge. Rounded, toothed leaves clasp the stem below flat clusters of small white flowers. Bloom opens in late summer and feeds many small bees and beneficial insects.

planting.note: Fall-sow or stratify; a dry-woodland species happy in dappled shade.

directions: Press seed onto the surface of well-drained woodland soil and keep barely damp. Stalkless leaves ride directly on the stems below airy heads of white bloom. Flowers arrive in late summer, thriving in the dry shade where many perennials struggle.

planting.note: Set out divisions or potted plants in spring; this selection comes true only from vegetative propagation.

directions: Plant divisions at the same depth they grew, in rich soil that stays reliably moist. This popular selection is more compact and sturdier than the wild species, with wine-red stems. Enormous mauve-pink domes open in mid to late summer and hum with butterflies.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring; a dwarf selection propagated only vegetatively.

directions: Set the crown level with the soil in a sunny bed and water through the first season. This shorter selection brings Joe-Pye scale down to fit smaller borders. Rosy-purple flower clusters top the plant from midsummer into fall over dark stems.

planting.note: Plant potted stock or divisions in spring; the most compact Joe-Pye for containers and small beds.

directions: Plant at soil level in a roomy container or front-of-border spot and keep evenly watered. This tidy dwarf holds mounds of purple-mauve bloom on a plant small enough for patio pots. Flowers open midsummer and draw butterflies right up to the first frosts.

planting.note: Divide or transplant in spring into moisture-retentive soil.

directions: Plant divisions at their original depth where the soil stays cool and damp. This robust selection carries large flattened heads of soft mauve-pink over strong upright stems. Bloom fills mid to late summer and remains a butterfly magnet until frost.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring; a rare white-flowered form propagated vegetatively.

directions: Set divisions into rich, consistently moist soil with plenty of overhead room. This tall selection swaps the usual mauve for creamy ivory-white flower domes. Bloom peaks in late summer, glowing against the dark hollow stems and drawing many pollinators.

planting.note: Divide or transplant in spring into deep, moisture-holding soil.

directions: Plant crowns level with the soil where roots stay cool and damp. This selection is prized for its deep purple stems that contrast with rich rosy flower heads. Large domed clusters open in mid to late summer and stand well into autumn.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring; a tall white-flowered selection grown vegetatively.

directions: Set divisions into deep, fertile soil that holds moisture, allowing space for a towering plant. Pure white flower heads crown stems that can reach eight feet in good conditions. Bloom arrives in late summer, providing a dramatic pale backdrop and steady nectar source.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring; the double form is sterile and must be propagated vegetatively.

directions: Plant divisions in damp, fertile ground near water or in a moisture-retentive border. Because the flowers are fully double they set no seed, so the dusky-pink heads last longer than the wild type. Bloom stretches from midsummer into autumn and holds its color without scattering seedlings.

planting.note: Set out plants after frost; grown for foliage and propagated from cuttings.

directions: Plant into warm, well-drained soil after the last frost and pinch early to build fullness. This selection is grown for its billowing emerald plumes of fine, ferny foliage rather than its flowers. The soft texture reads like a green cloud all season and pairs beautifully with bold-leaved plants.

planting.note: Plant potted stock or divisions in spring; a compact hybrid propagated vegetatively.

directions: Set the crown at soil level in a sunny bed and keep watered until established. This dwarf hybrid packs full-sized mauve-purple flower domes onto a plant half the usual height. Bloom fills mid to late summer on dark stems and needs no staking in most gardens.

planting.note: Formerly Eupatorium rugosum; plant divisions in spring for true chocolate foliage.

directions: Plant divisions in humus-rich soil where afternoon shade deepens the leaf color. New foliage emerges rich purple-brown and matures to bronzed green, a striking backdrop all season. Frothy white flowers cap the dark stems in early autumn and glow against the moody leaves.

planting.note: Start indoors in early spring or set out young plants after frost.

directions: Sow seed just under the surface and keep barely moist until sprouts appear, then thin to a hand's width apart. Give it lean, sharply drained soil and avoid overwatering, which causes rot. Bright chartreuse bracts light up the plant in mid to late spring, followed by tidy mounded foliage that reddens in fall.

planting.note: Plant out established seedlings once hard frosts have passed.

directions: Set plants in gritty, well-drained soil in full sun and shelter from harsh winter wind. Cut spent flowering stems to the base after bloom to encourage fresh growth from the crown. Domed heads of lime-green bracts appear from late winter into spring atop upright blue-green stems.

planting.note: Sow in place in spring in gritty soil; it self-sows readily.

directions: Give this trailing spurge sharp drainage and full sun, ideally along a wall or rock ledge where its stems can spill. It tolerates drought once rooted and resents wet feet. Chartreuse bracts crown the blue-scaled stems in early spring; note that it is invasive in some western states and its sap irritates skin.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in moisture-retentive soil.

directions: Plant in fertile, reliably moist soil in sun or light shade, where it spreads by underground runners into a colony. Cut stems back after flowering to keep growth tidy. Brick-red to orange bracts glow above green foliage in early summer, with good autumn leaf color.

planting.note: Set divisions in spring or fall; it colonizes shade well.

directions: One of the few spurges for dry shade, it forms spreading rosettes of dark evergreen leaves and tolerates poor soil under trees. Remove faded flower stems to the base to freshen the clump. Lime-green flower heads rise in spring; give it room, as it wanders by runners.

planting.note: Sow in place in spring; it self-seeds freely and comes true.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained soil in sun to part shade to bring out its deep purple foliage. Deadhead if you want to limit its enthusiastic self-sowing. Soft yellow-green flowers contrast with bronze-purple leaves in early summer, and foliage flushes red in autumn.

planting.note: Set out rooted plants after the last frost in free-draining soil.

directions: This hybrid forms a rounded evergreen mound with red-tinged new stems; give it sun and sharp drainage. Shear off spent flower heads after bloom to keep it compact. Lime-green bracts each centered with a dark red eye appear from spring into early summer.

planting.note: Plant nursery-grown stock after frost; propagate by cuttings only.

directions: Grown for its cream-edged, blue-green variegated foliage that flushes pink and red in cool weather, this cultivar needs full sun and excellent drainage. Wear gloves when trimming, as the milky sap irritates skin. Variegated lime and yellow flower heads appear in spring above the striking leaves.

planting.note: Start seed indoors and plant out into gritty soil after frost.

directions: Give this drought-tough spurge full sun and lean, fast-draining soil; it excels in dry gravel gardens. Arching blue-gray stems radiate from a central crown. Sulfur-yellow flower heads open in late winter to early spring and age to orange-red.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring; contain it, as it spreads aggressively.

directions: This fine-textured, needle-leaved spurge spreads by rhizomes to form a groundcover, so site it where roaming is welcome or restrict it. It thrives on neglect in poor, dry soil. Yellow-green bracts cover the feathery foliage in spring and often flush reddish later; note it is considered invasive in parts of North America.

planting.note: Set divisions or plants in spring in moisture-retentive soil.

directions: Plant in fertile, moist but well-drained soil in sun or dappled shade for an upright clump with white-veined leaves. Cut old stems to the ground in late autumn. Long-lasting yellow-green flower heads appear from midsummer well into autumn.

planting.note: Plant in spring in moist ground or beside water.

directions: Unusual among spurges, this species loves damp soil and even tolerates seasonal flooding, making it ideal for pond margins. Give it full sun for the sturdiest, bushiest growth. Bold yellow-green flower heads top the leafy stems in late spring, and foliage turns golden in fall.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring; propagate this cultivar vegetatively.

directions: This variegated selection carries cream-margined leaves washed with pink in cool weather and needs full sun and sharp drainage. Trim lightly after bloom to maintain the neat mound. Chartreuse-yellow bracts appear in spring against the pink-and-cream foliage.

planting.note: Plant rooted stock after frost in free-draining soil.

directions: Prized for lime-green flower heads studded with dark near-black nectar glands, this upright evergreen wants full sun and gritty soil. Cut flowered stems to the base once bloom fades to keep the clump vigorous. The showy heads appear from late winter into spring on blue-green stems.

planting.note: Set out plants after frost; propagate by cuttings only.

directions: Boldly variegated with white-margined gray-green leaves, this selection brightens sunny, well-drained borders. Wear gloves when cutting back, as the sap is irritating. Cream-and-green flower heads emerge in spring above the striking foliage.

planting.note: Start indoors and plant out into gritty soil after frost.

directions: A tough, drought-resistant spurge with blue-green foliage on red-tinted stems, it thrives in hot, dry, well-drained sites. Cut back spent stems after flowering. Yellow-green flower heads appear from early summer and continue over a long season.

planting.note: Start seed indoors; plant out after frost in dry soil.

directions: This fine-textured spurge forms an airy dome of narrow blue-green leaves and excels in gravel gardens with sharp drainage. It flowers over an exceptionally long season with little care. Delicate yellow-green flower heads open from early summer and keep coming into autumn.

planting.note: Plant out young shrubs after frost in a sheltered site.

directions: This large, shrubby, evergreen spurge grows fast into a rounded specimen and needs shelter from cold winds. Give it fertile, well-drained soil and space to spread. Honey-scented brown-green flower heads appear in late spring, followed by ornamental seed capsules.

planting.note: Plant rooted stock after frost in a sheltered, sunny spot.

directions: A vigorous shrubby hybrid with handsome whorls of dark green, pale-veined leaves, it makes a bold evergreen accent in mild gardens. Shelter it from hard frost and give it good drainage. Domed heads of honey-scented amber flowers appear in late spring and early summer.

planting.note: Start indoors in late winter; often grown as a cut flower.

directions: Grown widely by cut-flower growers, this spurge produces bright acid-green flower heads that last well in the vase. Sear cut stem ends to stop the milky sap from bleeding. Sow indoors for bloom the first year in warm climates; give it sun and average, well-drained soil.

planting.note: Plant in spring in cool, moist, well-drained soil.

directions: This Himalayan species has pale-veined leaves and prefers cool summers with reliably moist, humus-rich soil. Site it in sun or light shade and mulch to keep roots cool. Large yellow flower heads appear in early summer over a long season.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in moist, fertile soil.

directions: Grown as much for its brilliant coral-pink new shoots in spring as for its flowers, this species wants moist, rich soil in sun or part shade. The translucent red-veined young growth is a highlight of the early season. Yellow flower heads follow in mid to late summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in moist, well-drained soil.

directions: A tall, robust spurge with red-tinted stems and dark leaves marked by pale midribs, it makes a strong vertical statement in the border. Give it fertile, moisture-retentive soil in sun or light shade. Yellow-green flower heads with reddish tints appear through summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in fertile, well-drained soil.

directions: This upright species forms a clump of dark green, white-ribbed leaves and flowers freely over a long midsummer season. Give it sun and average garden soil that does not dry out badly. Bright yellow flower heads with horned glands stand well above the foliage.

planting.note: Plant in spring in moist woodland soil.

directions: A woodland-edge species that appreciates cool, moist, humus-rich soil in dappled shade. It forms a rounded clump and dies back over winter. Soft yellow-green flower heads appear in late spring, followed by distinctively warty seed capsules.

planting.note: Direct sow in spring; it self-sows and behaves as a biennial.

directions: This upright biennial has striking cross-paired blue-green leaves on a single stout stem and self-sows to reappear each year. Do not eat any part, as the seeds and sap are toxic despite the caper nickname. Green flower heads in the second summer are followed by round seed capsules; grow it in sun in average soil.

planting.note: Plant rooted stock after frost in a sheltered, sunny spot.

directions: This rare shrubby evergreen from the Azores forms a bold dome of glossy, pale-veined leaves and needs a frost-sheltered, well-drained site. Protect it from cold winds in marginal climates. Rounded heads of yellow-green flowers appear in spring.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in moist, fertile soil.

directions: Selected for its darker, more richly colored foliage and stems than the species, this spreading spurge shines in moist borders. Cut back after flowering to tidy the clump. Warm orange bracts glow above bronze-flushed leaves in early summer, with fiery autumn color.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in well-drained soil.

directions: Grown for its deep burgundy-purple winter foliage, this wood spurge colors best in sun but tolerates part shade. It self-sows, though seedlings vary in leaf color. Lime-green flower heads contrast beautifully with the dark leaves in spring.

planting.note: Plant rooted stock after frost in gritty, well-drained soil.

directions: A compact selection with soft, velvety gray-green leaves densely packed on the stems, it makes a handsome mound in dry sunny gardens. Cut spent flowering stems to the base after bloom. Lime-green flower heads appear in late winter and spring.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring; propagate this cultivar vegetatively.

directions: This compact cushion spurge carries rich burgundy-purple foliage all season, intensifying in cool weather and full sun. Give it lean, well-drained soil and avoid overwatering. Chartreuse-yellow bracts glow against the dark leaves in spring, and the whole plant reddens in autumn.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring; still spreads, so site with care.

directions: This selection emerges with reddish-purple new shoots that set off its feathery blue-green foliage, spreading by rhizomes as a groundcover. Restrict it in small gardens, as it still runs. Yellow-green bracts cover the fine foliage in spring.

planting.note: Plant rooted stock after frost in sharply drained soil.

directions: A more compact form of the upright myrtle spurge, this selection keeps the blue-gray whorled stems on a smaller frame ideal for troughs and gravel. Give it full sun and very sharp drainage. Yellow flower heads open in late winter to early spring and age to orange.

planting.note: Plant rooted stock after frost in gritty soil.

directions: A dwarf, compact characias that stays neat and rounded, well suited to smaller borders and containers. Give it full sun and free-draining soil, cutting spent stems to the base after bloom. Chunky lime-green flower heads appear in late winter and spring.

planting.note: Plant rooted stock after frost in well-drained soil.

directions: A dwarf Martin's spurge forming a tight evergreen dome with red-tinged stems, ideal for the front of a sunny, dry border. Trim off spent flower heads to keep it compact. Lime-green bracts with dark red eyes appear from spring into early summer.

planting.note: Plant rooted stock after frost in a warm, sunny spot.

directions: This bushy, long-flowering Mediterranean spurge produces fresh green foliage and blooms over an exceptionally extended season in mild climates. Give it full sun and free-draining soil. Yellow-green flower heads appear from late spring and continue into autumn.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in fertile, moist soil.

directions: Grown for its brilliant golden-yellow spring flower heads that seem to glow, this species wants sun and reliably moist, fertile soil. It forms a substantial clump over time. The vivid bloom display comes in late spring, held above fresh green foliage.

planting.note: Not recommended for planting; a noxious weed across much of North America.

directions: Listed here for identification only, this aggressively spreading species is a declared noxious weed across the northern United States and Canada and should not be planted. It colonizes by deep rhizomes and copious seed. Yellow-green flower heads appear in late spring; remove it where found rather than cultivating it.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in well-drained soil.

directions: This variegated cushion spurge edges its green leaves in creamy white for a lacy effect, brightening the front of a sunny border. Give it lean, sharply drained soil and full sun. Chartreuse bracts appear in spring above the neat variegated mound.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in fertile, well-drained soil.

directions: A tall, free-flowering selection whose German name means golden tower, it makes an upright column of white-ribbed leaves topped by bright bloom. Give it sun and soil that stays evenly moist. Golden-yellow flower heads stand well above the foliage through midsummer.

planting.note: Plant rooted stock after frost in gritty, well-drained soil.

directions: This variegated characias carries gray-green leaves crisply margined in creamy white, giving a cool silver effect in sunny borders. Wear gloves when trimming the irritant sap. Cream-and-green flower heads appear in spring above the bright foliage.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in moist, fertile soil.

directions: A vigorous spreading spurge with warm-toned bracts and good foliage color, spreading by runners in moist ground. Cut back after bloom to keep the colony tidy. Orange-red flower heads glow in early summer, with strong autumn leaf tints.

planting.note: Plant rooted stock after frost in sharply drained soil.

directions: A compact variegated characias with blue-gray leaves edged in creamy white, forming a tidy dome for smaller sunny gardens. Give it full sun and excellent drainage. Cream-and-lime flower heads appear from late winter into spring.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in moist, fertile soil.

directions: This clump-forming Himalayan spurge has long, pale-veined leaves and flowers over an extended season in cool, moist soil. Site it in sun or light shade and keep it from drying out. Greenish-yellow flower heads appear from late spring through summer.

planting.note: Plant rooted stock after frost in very well-drained soil.

directions: A dense, twiggy little subshrub from Mediterranean hillsides, it forms a spiny cushion that thrives in hot, dry, gravelly sites. Give it full sun and the sharpest drainage you can manage. Small yellow flower heads dot the mound in late spring and early summer.

planting.note: Plant rooted stock after frost in well-drained soil.

directions: A compact Martin's spurge selected for strongly red-flushed stems and new growth, making a colorful low evergreen mound. Give it full sun and free-draining soil, and trim spent heads after bloom. Lime-green bracts with dark eyes appear in spring above the reddish foliage.

planting.note: Direct sow in spring in lean, well-drained soil.

directions: An upright, sun-loving spurge tolerant of hot, dry, rocky sites, forming a clump of narrow gray-green leaves. Give it full sun and sharp drainage and leave it undisturbed once settled. Airy yellow-green flower heads appear through late spring and summer.

planting.note: Divide and replant clumps in spring so the bright new foliage colors up in cool weather.

directions: Set divisions in moist, humus-rich soil and shear the plant back if the golden leaves scorch in summer heat to force a fresh flush. This form is grown chiefly for its yellow foliage, brightest in spring and in dappled shade. Small cream flowers appear in summer but many gardeners clip them to keep the leaf color the main show.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in reliably moist ground for the best variegation.

directions: Give divisions rich, damp soil and light shade so the yellow-splashed leaves keep their contrast without burning. Remove any all-green shoots that revert to preserve the pattern. Creamy plumes open in early summer above the marbled foliage.

planting.note: Lift and divide clumps in spring, since this double form does not come true from seed.

directions: Plant divisions in consistently moist, fertile soil at a stream or pond margin. The fully double white flowers last longer than the single species and hold up well as long as roots stay wet. Cut spent stems after the midsummer bloom to tidy the clump.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring in wet ground with space to spread into a stand.

directions: Set divisions in moist, fertile soil where the vigorous rhizomes can naturalize at a pond edge or rain garden. This selection carries deeper rose-pink plumes than the wild type on stems six to eight feet tall. Deadhead or leave the coppery seed heads standing for late-season interest.

planting.note: Divide established clumps in spring and replant in deep, moisture-retentive soil.

directions: Plant in rich, wet soil in full sun to light shade and allow the rhizomes room to colonize. Large soft-pink flower heads top strong stems in early to midsummer. Keep the ground from drying out, as drought stress causes the foliage to brown at the edges.

planting.note: Start cold-stratified seed indoors and transplant around the last spring frost.

directions: Grow in cool, moist, humus-rich soil with afternoon shade in hot regions. The reddish stems carry flat clusters of rose-pink flowers in early to midsummer above deeply lobed maple-like leaves. Keep the soil evenly damp and divide crowded clumps every few years in spring.

planting.note: Divide clumps in spring and plant in moist, cool soil.

directions: Set divisions in rich, reliably moist ground with light shade in warm climates. This form is prized for white to pale-pink flowers accented by bright crimson stamens on dark red stems. Water freely through summer and cut back tired foliage after bloom.

planting.note: Sow stratified seed indoors and set plants out near the last frost, or divide in spring.

directions: Give this white-flowered form cool, moist, fertile soil and shelter from harsh afternoon sun. Clean white flower clusters float above lobed green leaves in early summer. Keep roots damp at all times and divide every three or four years to keep clumps vigorous.

planting.note: Divide clumps in spring or fall, as the double form must be propagated vegetatively.

directions: Plant divisions in average, well-drained soil in sun or light shade; this selection tolerates drier ground than most meadowsweets. The fully double buttonlike white flowers last well and look like tiny pompons on wiry stems in early summer. Shear after flowering and lift crowded clumps every few years to divide.

planting.note: Start cold-stratified seed indoors and plant out around the last frost in moist soil.

directions: Grow in cool, moist, fertile ground where the palmate leaves and pink-fading-to-white plumes can form a spreading clump. Keep soil damp through the growing season for best flowering in early to midsummer. Divide in spring to control spread and refresh older plantings.

planting.note: Divide and replant in spring in moist soil at the front of a border.

directions: This compact form suits the edge of a moist border or a bog garden where taller meadowsweets would overwhelm. Set divisions in cool, damp soil and keep them watered in dry spells. Small soft-pink flower clusters rise just above the neat mound of foliage in early summer.

planting.note: Divide clumps in spring and set into moist, rich soil.

directions: Plant divisions in cool, consistently moist ground in sun or part shade. This selection opens clear rose-pink plumes that soften to pale pink as they age in early to midsummer. Keep the soil damp and divide every few years to maintain flowering vigor.

planting.note: Sow cold-stratified seed indoors and transplant around the last frost into deep, wet soil.

directions: This bold Kamchatka species needs deep, rich, permanently moist soil and plenty of room to reach up to eight or ten feet. Large lobed leaves form a coarse clump topped by broad frothy plumes of white to pale-pink flowers in midsummer. Site it at the back of a bog or pondside planting where it will not be crowded.

planting.note: Divide and replant in spring in moist soil near the front of a border.

directions: This dwarf hybrid stays compact and works well at a pond edge or the front of a damp border. Plant divisions in cool, moisture-retentive soil and keep them watered through summer. Deep rose-pink plumes appear over bronze-tinged ferny foliage in early to midsummer and fade gently as they age.

planting.note: Divide clumps in spring and plant into consistently moist ground.

directions: Set divisions in rich, wet soil at a stream or pond margin in sun or light shade. Unlike the cream-flowered species, this form opens soft pink plumes in early to midsummer above the aromatic ferny leaves. Keep the ground reliably damp and cut back after flowering.

planting.note: Sow outdoors around the last spring frost or start indoors a few weeks earlier.

directions: Press seed lightly onto the surface since light aids germination and barely cover with fine soil. Give it lean, sharply drained ground and let the soil dry between waterings once established. Expect the first daisy-like red-and-yellow blooms in mid to late summer of the first year, continuing into fall if you deadhead.

planting.note: Direct sow after the danger of frost has passed and the soil has warmed.

directions: Scatter seed on warm, sandy soil and press it in without burying, as it needs light to sprout. Thrives on heat and neglect, tolerating poor ground and drought once up. This native annual blooms within a couple of months and self-sows freely to return the following year.

planting.note: Sow near the last frost or set out nursery plants in spring.

directions: Barely cover the seed and keep it lightly moist until sprouts appear. This cross between the two native species tolerates heat, drought, and lean soil, but resents soggy winter ground. Deadhead spent blooms through summer to keep the daisy flowers coming until frost.

planting.note: Sow in spring in well-drained soil; also grows readily from fall-sown seed.

directions: Sow shallowly into gritty, fast-draining soil and avoid overwatering. This prairie native carries fragrant, rounded rusty-orange flower heads on slender stalks above a low rosette. Blooms appear in late spring and early summer and are highly attractive to bees.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost in warm, sandy soil.

directions: Surface sow into sandy, well-drained ground and keep barely moist until germination. This southeastern native tolerates heat and humidity better than most blanketflowers. Look for yellow to purplish blooms from summer through fall; a variety with lavender flowers is sometimes grown.

planting.note: Sow near last frost or plant divisions in spring.

directions: Barely cover seed and keep lightly moist until it sprouts. This compact classic stays low and tidy, carrying red flowers rimmed in bright yellow. Deadhead often through summer for a long show on a plant well suited to the front of a border.

planting.note: Sow around last frost or set out plants in spring.

directions: Surface sow and press lightly, keeping evenly moist until germination. Rich wine-red daisies top taller stems that may need light support in rich ground. Deadhead through the season and blooms will continue from early summer until frost.

planting.note: Sow near last frost or plant in spring.

directions: Cover seed only lightly since light speeds sprouting. Large flowers open orange-red with contrasting yellow petal tips for a bold effect. Give it full sun and lean soil, and shear back midseason to refresh a tired clump.

planting.note: Set out divisions or nursery plants in spring.

directions: Plant in gritty, well-drained soil at the same depth as the nursery pot. This dwarf selection forms a neat cushion topped with red-and-yellow blooms held just above the foliage. Ideal for rock gardens and edging; deadhead to prolong flowering into fall.

planting.note: Start indoors about six weeks before last frost for first-year bloom.

directions: Sow indoors on the surface under light and transplant after frost. This award-winning dwarf blooms the first year from seed, with mahogany-red flowers edged in gold on a tidy mound. Deadhead for a continuous display all summer.

planting.note: Start indoors about six weeks before last frost.

directions: Surface sow indoors and keep warm and bright until seedlings are stout enough to move out. Soft apricot-yellow petals deepen toward the center on this compact, floriferous plant. Remove faded blooms to keep new buds forming through the season.

planting.note: Start indoors roughly six weeks before last frost.

directions: Press seed onto the surface of the mix and grow on under bright light. This dwarf bears deep red flowers with just a fine gold rim on the petal tips. It flowers the first year from seed and rewards regular deadheading with months of color.

planting.note: Start indoors several weeks before last frost for uniform first-year bloom.

directions: Sow on the surface under warmth and bright light for even germination. This award-winning F1 forms a rounded, uniform mound covered in clear yellow single daisies. It blooms early and heavily the first year and stands up well without staking.

planting.note: Start indoors weeks ahead of last frost.

directions: Surface sow and grow warm until seedlings can be moved outdoors after frost. Soft peach-toned flowers cover a compact, well-branched plant from early summer on. Deadhead lightly to encourage a fresh flush and keep the mound looking neat.

planting.note: Start indoors well before last frost.

directions: Sow on the surface and keep bright and warm through germination. This uniform F1 hybrid carries rich red flowers on a compact, weather-tolerant mound. Expect strong first-year flowering and remove spent heads to extend the display.

planting.note: Start indoors weeks before last frost.

directions: Surface sow under bright light and transplant out once frost has passed. Classic red-and-yellow bicolor daisies smother a tidy, uniform mound. It blooms the first year, stands up to summer weather, and flowers longest with regular deadheading.

planting.note: Start indoors ahead of last frost for first-year bloom.

directions: Sow on the surface and keep warm and bright until sturdy for transplant. This series stays very compact with large flowers held right above the foliage. Deep red blooms cover the plant from early summer; deadhead to keep them coming.

planting.note: Start indoors before last frost.

directions: Surface sow under light and grow on warm before setting out. Coppery-orange flowers with gold edges glow above the compact foliage. This tidy, early-blooming plant flowers heavily the first year and responds well to deadheading.

planting.note: Start indoors before last frost.

directions: Press seed onto the surface and keep bright until seedlings are ready to move out. Red-centered flowers finished with bright yellow petal tips cover this compact mound. It blooms early and long, and deadheading keeps the color going into fall.

planting.note: Start indoors weeks before last frost.

directions: Surface sow, keep warm, and grow under bright light before transplanting. Warm mango-orange blooms shade to yellow at the tips on a very compact plant. Deadhead through summer for the fullest, longest-lasting show.

planting.note: Set out nursery plants or divisions in spring; this cultivar is grown vegetatively.

directions: Plant in sharply drained soil at the depth of the container and avoid winter wet. Its unusual petals are fluted into little red trumpets tipped with yellow, giving a distinctive fringed look. Deadhead regularly and it will bloom from early summer well into fall.

planting.note: Set out plants in spring; propagated vegetatively, not from seed.

directions: Plant into lean, well-drained ground and keep the crown from sitting wet in winter. The trumpet-shaped petals show fiery red-orange with yellow tips for a warm, tubular effect. Shear off spent flowers to keep the plant blooming steadily through the season.

planting.note: Set out plants in spring; grown from cuttings or divisions.

directions: Set into fast-draining soil at the same level as the nursery pot. This selection carries the fluted trumpet petals in soft, glowing yellow tones. Give it full sun and lean conditions, and deadhead to keep the tubular blooms coming all summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions or potted stock in spring.

directions: Plant in gritty, well-drained soil in full sun and go easy on water once settled. Soft peachy-orange petals fade to lemon-yellow at the tips for a gentler color than the usual red. Deadhead through summer to keep this long-blooming selection flowering into fall.

planting.note: Set out plants in spring; propagated vegetatively.

directions: Plant in full sun and well-drained soil, spacing generously for its larger size. Big golden-yellow daisies with fluted, tubular petal tips top tall stems. Deadhead to prolong bloom and cut back after the first heavy flush to tidy the clump.

planting.note: Plant divisions or nursery stock in spring.

directions: Set into lean, well-drained soil and avoid soggy winter conditions. Rich rusty-orange flowers glow on this taller German selection. Deadhead frequently and it will flower reliably from early summer through fall.

planting.note: Sow near last frost or plant in spring.

directions: Barely cover the seed and keep lightly moist until it germinates. This is the all-yellow counterpart to the classic Goblin, staying low and compact. Solid golden daisies cover the mound all summer; deadhead to keep them coming.

planting.note: Set out nursery plants in spring; propagated vegetatively.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained soil at the level of the pot. The flowers carry fluted, tubular petals in warm orange and yellow tones for a frilled, pinwheel look. Deadhead regularly to keep this floriferous selection blooming through the season.

planting.note: Set out plants in spring; grown from cuttings or divisions.

directions: Set into sharply drained soil and keep the crown dry over winter. Fluted, tubular petals in soft yellow give the blooms a fringed pinwheel appearance. Full sun and steady deadheading bring a long summer-to-fall display.

planting.note: Plant nursery stock in spring; propagated vegetatively.

directions: Plant in a sunny spot with fast-draining soil at the same depth as the pot. This very compact selection forms a dense cushion of bright orange and yellow blooms. It works well in containers and edging; deadhead to keep the flowers coming.

planting.note: Set out plants in spring; propagated vegetatively.

directions: Plant in full sun and well-drained soil, spacing to allow good air movement. Bright red flowers cover a rounded, well-branched mound over a long season. Remove spent blooms to keep new buds forming from early summer into fall.

planting.note: Start indoors weeks before last frost for first-year flowering.

directions: Surface sow under bright light and grow on warm until ready to transplant. This very dwarf, early series carries solid red blooms on a compact base. It flowers quickly from seed and suits pots and small spaces; deadhead for a longer show.

planting.note: Start indoors ahead of last frost.

directions: Press seed onto the surface and keep bright and warm through germination. Clear yellow flowers cover this compact, early-blooming dwarf. It flowers the first year from seed and rewards deadheading with continued color.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost, or start indoors for earlier bloom.

directions: Surface sow into warm soil since the seed needs light to germinate. This award-winning annual bears fully double, ball-shaped brick-red flowers on compact plants. It blooms in about two months and keeps going all summer with occasional deadheading.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost or start early indoors.

directions: Scatter seed on warm soil and press in without covering. Compact plants carry rounded, fully double golden-yellow flowers. This heat-loving annual blooms quickly and continues through summer; deadhead to prolong the show.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost in warm, well-drained soil.

directions: Surface sow onto warm soil and keep barely moist until germination. This old double-flowered strain produces globe-shaped blooms in a mix of red, orange, and yellow tones. It thrives in heat and poor soil and makes an excellent, long-lasting cut flower.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost or start indoors for earlier color.

directions: Press seed onto warm soil, leaving it uncovered to catch the light it needs. This very dwarf annual forms a neat mound of red-and-yellow bicolor daisies. It blooms early and heavily, making a tidy edging or container plant that flowers until frost.

planting.note: Direct sow after frost in warm soil.

directions: Surface sow into warm, sandy soil and do not bury the seed. This tall firewheel bears bright red flowers with contrasting yellow bands, excellent for cutting. Heat and drought tolerant, it blooms all summer and self-sows to return the next year.

planting.note: Direct sow in spring on lean, well-drained ground in full sun.

directions: Scatter seed shallowly on gritty, low-fertility soil and press it into contact rather than burying it deep. Once established it tolerates drought well and can spread by runners, so give it room in a meadow or dry bank. Frothy plumes of honey-scented yellow flowers open through summer and are a classic dye and old-time mattress-stuffing plant.

planting.note: Direct sow in spring in sun or light shade on average soil.

directions: Sow seed thinly and barely cover it, thinning seedlings to give this sprawling plant support from neighbors. Its lax stems scramble through meadow grasses and benefit from a wild, unmown setting rather than a tidy border. Clouds of small white four-petaled flowers appear across summer and are a magnet for small pollinators.

planting.note: Sow in fall in moist shade or cold-stratify seed for spring.

directions: Chill the seed over winter and sow in rich, moist woodland soil where it can weave among ferns and shade perennials. The reclining stems root as they wander, making a soft groundcover in damp forest gardens. Small greenish-white flowers appear in threes through summer, and the dried foliage carries the same sweet vanilla-hay scent as its European cousin sweet woodruff.

planting.note: Direct sow in spring in sun or partial shade on well-drained soil.

directions: Sow seed shallowly on average soil and let the plants knit together in a meadow planting. Taller and more erect than hedge bedstraw, its whorled leaves climb sturdy stems that still appreciate the support of surrounding grasses. Airy sprays of white flowers open in summer and dry well for arrangements.

planting.note: Sow in fall or stratify seed for spring in dappled shade.

directions: Give the seed a cold, moist period and grow the plants in light woodland shade with humus-rich soil. Slender arching stems carry blue-green whorled leaves and sway gracefully in the slightest breeze. Loose panicles of tiny white flowers hover like a fine mist above the foliage in early to mid summer.

planting.note: Sow in spring on lean, acid soil in sun or light shade.

directions: Sow seed on gritty, acidic soil and avoid rich feeding, which this plant of heaths and lean turf dislikes. Its low mat of prostrate stems hugs the ground and makes a fine filler between rocks or in short wildflower turf. Small white flowers dot the mat in summer, tinting the whole patch a soft creamy white.

planting.note: Sow in spring in consistently wet ground at a pond or bog edge.

directions: Sow seed in wet, mucky soil at the margin of a pond, ditch, or rain garden where the roots stay damp. The weak scrambling stems lean on rushes and sedges for support in a naturalistic wetland planting. Delicate white flowers open across summer, adding a lacy texture among taller marsh plants.

planting.note: Direct sow in spring on dry, sunny, well-drained soil.

directions: Sow seed shallowly on sharply drained, gritty soil and keep the site sunny and lean. The narrow blue-gray whorled leaves give this dry-ground bedstraw a cool, glaucous look well suited to gravel and rock gardens. Branching sprays of small white flowers appear in early summer above the wiry stems.

planting.note: Sow in spring on thin, calcareous soil in full sun.

directions: Sow seed on lean, chalky ground and keep competition low, as this fine-stemmed plant belongs to short, species-rich turf. Its delicate needle-like leaves and threadlike stems form a low, wiry cushion. Tiny white flowers open in early summer, best appreciated up close in a rock or gravel garden.

planting.note: Sow in spring on shallow, limestone-rich soil in full sun.

directions: Sow seed on thin, alkaline soil over limestone and give it the sharp drainage of a rock garden or scree. The prostrate mat of tiny bristle-edged leaves stays tight and low against the ground. Small white flowers appear in early summer, dotting the cushion in a plant prized for limestone wildflower turf.

planting.note: Sow in spring in wet, base-rich soil at a bog or fen edge.

directions: Sow seed in permanently moist, mineral-rich ground such as a fen margin or damp meadow. The rough, backward-hooked stems scramble weakly through surrounding sedges and grasses. Small white flowers open through summer among the fine whorled leaves, best used in a naturalistic wetland planting.

planting.note: Start seed indoors and set plants out around the last spring frost into warm, well-drained soil.

directions: Sow seed shallowly, barely covered, as light aids germination, and keep the mix warm and lightly moist until sprouts appear. Give plants lean, sharply drained soil and full sun; avoid overwatering, which causes floppy growth and rot. Airy wands of white-to-pink flowers open from late spring until frost, dancing above the foliage.

planting.note: Plant nursery divisions or potted starts in spring after frost danger passes.

directions: Set plants at the same depth they grew in their pots and firm the soil, then water in well to settle roots. This vigorous white selection needs full sun and excellent drainage; shear it back by a third in midsummer to renew the display. Slender pink buds open to pure-white blooms that flutter on wiry stems from early summer to autumn.

planting.note: Plant potted starts in spring into a sunny, well-drained bed.

directions: Plant into lean, gritty soil and avoid rich amendments that promote weak stems. Deep pink buds and rosy flowers sit above maroon-flecked foliage; cut spent wands back to encourage rebloom. Flowers appear from late spring through fall and are best in poor, dry ground.

planting.note: Set potted plants out in spring once the soil has warmed.

directions: Plant this compact selection in full sun with sharp drainage and space it for good air movement. The burgundy-tinted foliage sets off deep-pink to crimson flowers on a tidy mound. Blooms carry from early summer into fall; trim lightly to keep the plant neat and flowering.

planting.note: Plant potted starts in spring into full sun.

directions: Give this variegated form full sun to keep the gold leaf margins bright and the habit sturdy. Plant in well-drained soil and water sparingly once established. White flowers blushed with pink float above cream-edged foliage from summer to frost.

planting.note: Start seed indoors well before the last frost; this strain flowers the first year from seed.

directions: Press seed onto the surface without covering, since light triggers germination, and keep warm and evenly moist. This seed-grown, first-year-flowering strain forms a compact mound covered in white blooms with pink tints. Expect flowers within about three to four months of sowing and continuing until frost.

planting.note: Plant potted starts in spring into a sunny, sharply drained spot.

directions: Plant in full sun and lean soil, keeping water modest to prevent flopping. The bicolor blooms are white with a rose-pink picotee edge that catches the eye up close. Flowers open from early summer to fall; shear midseason for a fresh flush.

planting.note: Start seed indoors and transplant out around the last frost.

directions: Surface-sow seed and keep warm and bright until germination, then grow on in full sun. Pinkish buds open to clean white flowers on airy, slender stems. Bloom runs from early summer through autumn in poor, well-drained ground.

planting.note: Plant potted starts after frost; this compact series suits containers and front borders.

directions: Set plants in full sun with good drainage, spacing them for airflow in beds or pots. This compact Belleza selection carries deep-pink flowers over dark-tinged foliage without needing much water. Blooms flush repeatedly from late spring to fall.

planting.note: Plant potted starts after frost into sun; good for containers.

directions: Plant this compact form in full sun and lean, well-drained soil for the sturdiest habit. Soft pink-and-white blooms cover the tidy mound over a long season. Flowers appear from late spring through frost with little care.

planting.note: Plant potted starts after frost into full sun.

directions: Grow in full sun with sharp drainage to keep this compact plant dense and free-flowering. Rich, dark-pink blossoms open along short wands above the foliage. Enjoy continuous bloom from late spring until fall.

planting.note: Plant potted starts in spring into a sunny, well-drained bed.

directions: Plant in full sun and gritty soil, then water only during establishment. This compact white form makes a fountain of pure-white flowers on slim stems. Bloom continues from early summer to frost with occasional shearing.

planting.note: Start seed indoors; this seed series is uniform and compact.

directions: Surface-sow seed and keep warm and moist until sprouts emerge, then grow on in bright light. This compact seed strain produces rose-pink flowers on a well-branched, uniform plant. Blooms carry from summer to frost in full sun and lean soil.

planting.note: Start seed indoors and transplant out near the last frost.

directions: Press seed onto the surface for light exposure and keep warm until germination. This uniform seed strain bears white flowers on a compact, tidy plant. Expect bloom from midsummer to fall in a sunny, well-drained spot.

planting.note: Start seed indoors before the last frost for first-year bloom.

directions: Surface-sow and keep warm and lightly moist until seedlings appear, then grow in full light. Soft blush-pink flowers cover this compact, uniform seed selection. Bloom runs from summer to frost in lean, sharply drained soil.

planting.note: Start seed indoors; this dwarf series is well suited to pots.

directions: Surface-sow seed under warm, bright conditions and avoid burying it. This dwarf seed series stays neat and blooms early, ideal for containers and edging. Pink flowers appear the first season and continue into fall.

planting.note: Start seed indoors early for first-year flowering in pots or beds.

directions: Sow seed on the surface and keep warm until germination, then grow on in full light. This compact, early seed strain carries white flowers over a dense mound. Flowering starts the first year and lasts into autumn in sunny, well-drained soil.

planting.note: Start seed indoors early; dwarf habit suits containers.

directions: Press seed onto the mix surface and hold warm and moist until sprouting. This dwarf seed selection shows deep rosy-red flowers on a tidy plant. Bloom begins the first season and runs into fall in full sun.
